learningGoal: How to understand that sharing equally is the same as dividing
Why division undoes multiplication

screens.title: Can you share 24 sweets equally?
screens.body:
Charlie is sharing sweets at a party. There are **24 sweets** and **6 groups**. How many does each group get?
Sharing equally IS dividing! Let's see why...

screens.title: Sharing = Dividing
screens.body: Sharing equally and dividing are the **same thing** — and you can always check your answer by multiplying back. Tap to see why!

steps.why: How many in each group?
Multiplication is the reverse!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Charlie now has **30 sweets** to share equally between **5 groups**. How many does each group get?

screens.title: Sharing and dividing — the same thing!
screens.body: Whenever you share things equally, you're **dividing**. And you can always **check with multiplication** — it's the reverse!

steps.why: Total ÷ number of groups = amount in each group

steps.text: Division is the opposite of multiplication

steps.why: Groups × each = total. If it matches, you're right! ✓

learningGoal: How to understand that sharing equally is the same as dividing
Why you must check division with multiplication

screens.title: Can you spot Evie's mistake?
screens.body:
Evie is sharing biscuits at a bake sale. There are **36 biscuits** shared between **9 groups**.
Evie says each group gets **6**. Something's not right...

screens.title: Check with multiplication!
screens.body: The quickest way to catch a division mistake is to **multiply back**. If the answer is right, you should get the original total. Tap to see what happens!

steps.why: This doesn't equal 36 — wrong!
This matches!

screens.title: Now you try a different one!
screens.body: 28 biscuits shared equally between 7 groups. How many in each group?

screens.title: Always check your division!
screens.body: The best way to avoid mistakes in division is to **check with multiplication**. If groups × each = total, you're right!

steps.why: Total ÷ groups = each
Groups × your answer should equal the total

steps.text: If it doesn't match, try again!
steps.why: A quick check catches silly mistakes every time. ✓

learningGoal: How to handle remainders in the bus stop method
How to write the remainder (the amount left over after dividing) at the end of your answer

screens.title: 157 ÷ 4 — will it divide exactly?
screens.body:
Miss Carter is sharing coloured pencils between tables. There are **157 pencils** to share between **4** groups.
Sometimes numbers don't divide perfectly — there's a bit **left over**. That's called a remainder (the amount left over after dividing). Let's work through it!

bodyParts.content: Let's work through **157 ÷ 4** digit by digit. The **divisor** (the number you divide by) is **4**. We carry any remainder to the next column.
**Step 1:** 4 doesn't go into 1 — write 0 above and carry 1.

**Step 2:** The carried 1 makes it **15**.
15 ÷ 4 = 3 remainder 3. Write 3 above.

**Step 3:** The carried 3 makes it **37**.
37 ÷ 4 = 9 remainder 1. That's our final remainder!

**Answer: 39 remainder 1** ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **215 ÷ 4**? Use the bus stop method to work it out.

interaction.question: What is 215 ÷ 4?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **215 ÷ 4 = 53 remainder 3**. Each group gets **53 pencils** with **3** left over! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 215 ÷ 4 = **53 remainder 3**. Try going digit by digit using the bus stop method.

learningGoal: How to spot when a remainder is too big
Why the remainder must be smaller than the divisor (the number you divide by)

screens.title: Can you spot Luca's mistake?
screens.body:
Luca is dividing counters between groups in maths class.
Luca says: **197 ÷ 6 = 31 r11**.
Look at that remainder (what is left over after dividing)... does something seem wrong?

screens.title: The remainder must be smaller than the divisor!
screens.body: Luca stopped one step early and got a remainder of 11, which is bigger than 6. Here is the **golden rule**: the remainder must always be less than the **divisor** (the number you're dividing by). Tap to compare the wrong and right answers.

steps.why: 11 is bigger than 6 — keep dividing!
5 is less than 6 — that's correct!
Multiply back and add the remainder to check

interaction.question: What is 233 ÷ 6?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **233 ÷ 6 = 38 remainder 5**. The remainder 5 is less than 6 — perfect! ✓

learningGoal: How to divide by a 2-digit number using long division
How to estimate, multiply, subtract, and move to the next number

screens.title: 432 ÷ 18 — dividing by a bigger number!
screens.body:
Mrs Barnes is sharing out books for the school library. There are **432 books** — that's the dividend (the number being divided) — split into groups of **18** — that's the divisor (the number you divide by).
When we divide by a **2-digit number**, we use **long division**. It's like the bus stop method, but with an extra step — subtracting and moving to the next number!

bodyParts.content: Let's work through **432 ÷ 18** step by step. The **divisor** (the number you divide by) is **18**. Any leftover at the end is the **remainder** (what's left over after dividing).
**1. ESTIMATE:** Look at the first two digits: **43**. How many 18s fit? **2** (because 18 × 2 = 36).
**2. SUBTRACT:** 43 − 36 = **7**. This is what's left over.
**3. MOVE TO THE NEXT NUMBER:** Put the next digit next to 7 to get **72**.
**4. REPEAT:** How many 18s fit into 72? **4** (because 18 × 4 = 72). Subtract: 72 − 72 = 0.

interaction.question: What is 378 ÷ 18?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 378 ÷ 18 = **21**. Try estimating: how many 18s fit into the first two digits?

learningGoal: How to spot misaligned digits in long division
Why the answer number must go above the right column

screens.title: Can you spot Jake's mistake?
screens.body:
Jake is working out how many bags of crisps to order.
Jake says: **468 ÷ 12 = 93**.
Does that look right to you?

screens.title: Watch where you write each number!
screens.body: Jake wrote the answer digits in the wrong columns — 9 in the tens and 3 in the ones, instead of 3 in the tens and 9 in the ones. Keeping your columns lined up is everything! Tap to see the wrong and right answers.

bodyParts.content: Jake wrote **93** but the correct answer is **39**.

steps.why: wrote the answer digits in the wrong columns — 9 in the tens and 3 in the ones, instead of 3 in the tens and 9 in the ones

interaction.question: What is 348 ÷ 12?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **348 ÷ 12 = 29**. You kept those numbers in the right place! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 348 ÷ 12 = **29**. Remember to line up each number carefully!

learningGoal: How to decide whether to round up, round down, or give the remainder as a fraction
Why the context of a word problem changes what you do with the remainder

screens.title: What do you do with the leftovers?
screens.body:
Mrs Ahmed is booking coaches for a school trip. There are **157 children** and groups of **48**.
157 ÷ 48 = 3 remainder (what's left over) 13.
But what does the remainder **mean** in real life? Do you round up, round down, or use a fraction?

steps.why: But the answer to the real question isn't just 3...

screens.title: What do you do with remainder 13?

bodyParts.content:
**157 ÷ 48 = 3 remainder 13**

Getting the maths right is only half the job — you also need to decide what the remainder **means** in real life.
There are **three** things you can do with a remainder:

steps.text: Round UP — need an extra group
steps.why: E.g. coaches for a trip — you can't leave anyone behind!

steps.text: Round DOWN — can't use the leftovers
steps.why: E.g. cutting ribbon — the leftover bit is too short

steps.text: Give as a fraction — share the remainder
steps.why: E.g. sharing pizzas — cut the last one up!

bodyParts.content:
In this problem, we **round up** because you need an extra coach for the remaining 13 children — you can't leave them behind!

Answer: **4 coaches** ✓

screens.title: Think about the context!
screens.body: Mrs Ahmed is booking minibuses for a swimming gala. There are **95 pupils** and each group holds **20**. How many minibuses are needed?

interaction.question: How many minibuses?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The answer is **5 minibuses**. You worked out you need to **round up** because you need an extra minibus for the remaining 15 pupils — you can't leave them behind! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Think about it: you need an extra minibus for the remaining 15 pupils — you can't leave them behind! So the answer is **5 minibuses**.

screens.title: Read the question — then decide!
screens.body: The maths gives you an answer and a remainder. But what do you do with it?

steps.text: Step 1: Do the division — find the answer and remainder
steps.why: The maths part comes first
The question tells you what to do with the remainder

steps.text: Step 3: Round UP for people problems
steps.why: Coaches, tables, minibuses — everyone needs a seat!

steps.text: Step 4: Round DOWN for 'full' or 'complete' problems
steps.why: Full shelves, complete boxes — no room for leftovers

steps.text: Step 5: Give as a fraction for sharing problems
steps.why: Pizzas, cakes — you can split the leftovers! ✓

learningGoal: How to spot when someone interprets a remainder incorrectly
When to round up vs round down in division word problems

screens.title: Did Freya get this right?
screens.body:
Freya is working out how many minibuses are needed for a choir trip.
85 ÷ 15 = 5 r10. The r means remainder (the amount left over after dividing).
Freya says the answer is **5 minibuses**. Is that correct?

steps.why: Think carefully about what happens to the remainder...

screens.title: Read the question carefully!
screens.body: Freya said 5 minibuses, but that only fits 75 people — the other 10 singers would be left behind! You need to round up to 6.. The key is thinking about what happens to the **remainder** (the amount left over after dividing) in the real world. Tap to see the correct reasoning.

steps.why: The maths is right
But the interpretation is wrong!
Think: what happens to the leftover 10 singers?

screens.title: Your turn with a new problem!
screens.body: Freya is working out how many boats are needed for a river trip. There are **70 children** and each group holds **12**. How many boats?

interaction.question: How many boats?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **6 boats** is correct. You thought about what happens to the remainder! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is **6 boats**. Remember: think about whether the leftover 10 children need to be accounted for!

subConcepts.name: Estimating Before Dividing, Checking with Multiplication

learningGoal: How to estimate (make a rough guess at) a division answer by rounding
How to check your answer using multiplication

screens.title: Estimate first, check after!
screens.body:
Emily is estimating how many bags of sweets to buy for a party. Before doing the full division of **372 ÷ 6**, a quick estimate helps you know roughly what to expect!
**How to estimate:** Round to a nearby number that divides easily. This stops silly mistakes before they happen.

screens.title: Round the dividend to a friendly number
screens.body: The trick is to round the dividend (the number being divided — here it's **372**) to a **friendly number** that the **divisor** (the number you divide by — here it's **6**) goes into easily. After dividing, check by multiplying back — any **remainder** (the amount left over) gets added on. Tap to see the method!

steps.why: Round 372 to 360
Now do the full calculation
Multiply back to verify

screens.title: What's a good estimate?
screens.body: Before dividing **372 ÷ 6**, what's a good estimate?

interaction.question: Approximately what is 372 ÷ 6?
interaction.correctFeedback: Great estimate! 360 ÷ 6 = about **60**. The exact answer is 62 — nice and close! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Round 372 to 360. Then 360 ÷ 6 = **60**. That's a good estimate!

screens.title: Estimate and check — your safety net!

learningGoal: How to use estimating to avoid big mistakes
When your answer should be close to your estimate

screens.title: Does 305 seem right?
screens.body:
Grace is working out how many packets of seeds to buy. Grace calculated **315 ÷ 9 = 305**.
But wait — a quick estimate would have caught this mistake instantly! Let's see how...

steps.why: Does this seem reasonable?
305 is way off from 30!

screens.title: Estimating catches big mistakes!
screens.body: A 10-second estimate catches big mistakes instantly! Tap to see how.

steps.why: 270 divides easily by 9
So the real answer should be close to 30
305 is nowhere near 30 — something went wrong!

screens.title: Now try a different one!
screens.body: What is **378 ÷ 9**? (Hint: estimate first — it should be close to 40!)

interaction.question: What is 378 ÷ 9?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **378 ÷ 9 = 42**. Close to our estimate of 40 — makes sense! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 378 ÷ 9 = **42**. It's close to our estimate of 40, as expected.

steps.why: The answer should be close to this!

screens.title: Estimate first — always!
screens.body: A quick estimate takes 10 seconds and can save you from big mistakes:

steps.text: Round to a friendly number and divide
steps.why: This gives you a rough answer in seconds.
Use the bus stop or long division method.

steps.text: Compare your answer to the estimate
steps.why: If they're close, you're probably right. If not — check again! ✓

learningGoal: How to spot when a word problem needs division
How to pick out the right numbers from a word problem

screens.title: What type of calculation is hiding here?
screens.body:
A school has 384 pupils. They are split into 8 equal houses for sports day.
How many pupils are in each house?

The word "**equal**" or "**each**" in a problem is often a clue that you need to **divide**. Can you spot the two key numbers?

steps.why: These words tell us it's a division problem!

screens.title: Pick out the numbers!
screens.body: The secret to word problems is finding the **total** and the **group size** hiding in the text. Tap to see how to pick them out!

steps.why: This is the big number being shared or split
This is how many groups (or the size of each group)
That's our answer!

screens.title: Can you solve this one?
screens.body:
A school has 216 pupils. They are divided into 6 equal teams for a quiz.
How many pupils are in each team?

interaction.question: What is 216 ÷ 6?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **216 ÷ 6 = 36**. You spotted it was a division problem and picked out the right numbers! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 216 ÷ 6 = **36**. Look for the total and the group size — then divide!

learningGoal: How to avoid dividing the wrong way round
Why the bigger number usually goes first in division

screens.title: Sam set up the division wrong!
screens.body:
A farmer has 156 eggs. He puts them into boxes of 12.
How many boxes does he need?
Sam wrote: **12 ÷ 156**. But that gives 0 point something! What went wrong?

steps.why: That's the wrong way round!

screens.title: The total always goes FIRST!
screens.body: Sam divided the small number by the big number (12 ÷ 156) instead of the big number by the small number (156 ÷ 12). In division, the **total** always goes first, and the **divisor** (the number you divide by) goes second. Tap to compare the wrong and right setups.

steps.why: Small ÷ big doesn't make sense here!
Total ÷ group size = number of groups

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
A shop has 192 bottles of juice. They go into packs of 8.
How many packs are there?

interaction.question: What is 192 ÷ 8?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **192 ÷ 8 = 24**. You got the numbers the right way round! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 192 ÷ 8 = **24**. Remember: the total goes first!
